body.u: Looking back over the past fifty eight years of my life overall I am pleased with what I achieved. I became a teacher when I was twenty three. From then on I was happy I had achieved what I wanted to be as a person and was living pretty comfortably. But things in those days were not easy to come by, mind you with what I had been told when I was sixteen I was getting things easy. I didn’t think so.

When I was about thirteen and had joined the local comprehensive school, the only question which came up was ‘what are you going to do when you leave school’. Of course then I had no idea what I was going to do. Halfway through the first year of the school we had to think very seriously what we were going to do in the coming last two years of our official school life. This same as a not too welcome surprise. I mean nothing of this sort of division making had been done in the previous school. It was suddenly sprangsprung upon me.

Perhaps I was fortunate in having parent that helped me make the right decision. I did make the correct division in my eyes. In the following two years my work was of a slightly above average standard.

But the last year of compulsory schooling thing started becoming more worrying. I was on one hand hoping violently that I would get a good result on my exams so I could carry on in the sixth form and eventually go to college. For I had made up my mind to be a trader in the end of the last year of compulsory schooling. But on the other hand I was dreading the exam results to be negative. For that then meant I would have to leave school and find work. This was particularly unpleasant because unemployment at that time was terrible and secondly I would never achiveachieve another substitute to what I really wanted to be. This carried on for about eight months before the exam results had come through.

I had passed some of the exams but unfortunately failed some as well. I persuaded my parents to let me stay on at school where I managed to get to college everything was fine. It was just the getting off the ground part of the school which held you in torment.

As the years progressed on, I saw what I had hoped it would be like when I was at the end of my compulsory school life. Young people were being prepared for what they had to decide on in their later life in the last year of the middle schools. There was also a greater chance for young people to stay on at school for their benefit. As unemployment came down more employees gave more of an opportunity of work for them instead of the usual letter of disappiontmentdisappointment.

The pupils I used to teach never seemed bothered by the worries I had. Perhaps the generations had changed their view or was it that the worries did not exist anymore. I do not really know, but I hope for the youngstersyoungsters' sake that the latter reason is correct.

The money of a teacher at the early stages was not an amount one could boast about. But I enjoyed my job and as the years went by so did the money. Until now retired I think that it was worth it, but should anyone have to go through the worry of his or her future at such a late time?
